name: Job Scheduler
overview:
A distributed job scheduler executes tasks at specified times or on recurring schedules (cron-style) across a
cluster of worker nodes. The system must guarantee at-least-once execution (no missed jobs), support
exactly-once execution for idempotent jobs, handle worker failures gracefully, provide job prioritization, and
scale to millions of scheduled jobs. The scheduler must be fault-tolerant — if the scheduler itself fails, jobs must
not be lost.

inScope: Schedule one-time jobs at a specific time
Job timeout and retry with backoff

items: Scheduling precision ±100ms; jobs must not fire more than once (at-least-once with idempotency)
Fault tolerant: scheduler leader failure triggers re-election within 10 seconds; no jobs lost
99.9% availability for job dispatch; missed jobs during downtime must be caught up on restart
Horizontally scalable worker pool: auto-scale based on Kafka consumer lag
Job execution history retained for audit and debugging

entities.name: Job
entities.schema:
{
  job_id: UUID,
  name: string,
  schedule: string,  // cron expression or ISO8601 datetime
  payload: JSON,
  priority: enum(HIGH|MEDIUM|LOW),
  timeout_seconds: int,

  max_retries: int,
  retry_backoff: enum(LINEAR|EXPONENTIAL),
  status: enum(PENDING|RUNNING|COMPLETED|FAILED|CANCELLED)
}

entities.name: JobExecution
entities.schema:
{
  execution_id: UUID,
  job_id: UUID,
  worker_id: string,
  started_at: timestamp,
  completed_at: timestamp,
  status: enum(RUNNING|COMPLETED|FAILED|TIMED_OUT),
  result: JSON,
  error: string
}

endpoints.request: { name, schedule, payload, priority, timeout_seconds, max_retries }

approaches.title: Single-Node Cron
approaches.description: Run a single cron daemon that reads jobs from a DB and executes them. Single point of failure. If the cron daemon crashes, all jobs are missed. Cannot scale beyond one machine.
approaches.tradeoff: Single point of failure; not scalable.

approaches.title: Polling-Based Scheduler
approaches.description: Multiple scheduler nodes poll the DB every second for jobs due in the next minute. The first scheduler to claim a job (using optimistic locking) executes it. Polling creates DB load. Race conditions between schedulers require careful locking. Polling interval limits scheduling precision.
approaches.tradeoff: DB polling load; race conditions; 1-second minimum precision.

approaches.title: Time-Partitioned Priority Queue
approaches.description: Jobs are stored in a time-partitioned priority queue (Redis sorted set, score = next_run_timestamp). A scheduler cluster uses leader election (Zookeeper) to elect a single leader that dequeues due jobs and dispatches them to worker queues. Workers pull jobs from priority queues (HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW) in Kafka. Job claims use distributed locks to prevent double execution. The scheduler leader is a lightweight process; if it fails, a new leader is elected within 10 seconds.
approaches.tradeoff: Leader election adds complexity; Redis sorted set has O(log N) operations.

approaches.title: No Deduplication
approaches.description: Execute jobs without deduplication. If a worker crashes mid-execution, the job is retried and may execute twice. For non-idempotent jobs (e.g., send email, charge credit card), double execution causes real harm.
approaches.tradeoff: Double execution for non-idempotent jobs is unacceptable.

approaches.title: Idempotency Key + Distributed Lock
approaches.description: Each job execution is assigned a unique execution_id. Workers acquire a distributed lock (Redis SETNX) with the execution_id before starting. The lock TTL equals the job timeout. If a worker crashes, the lock expires and another worker can claim the job. The new worker checks if the job was already completed (by querying the execution DB) before executing. For truly non-idempotent jobs, the application layer must implement idempotency (e.g., check if email was already sent before sending).
approaches.tradeoff: Distributed lock adds latency; lock TTL must be tuned carefully.

components.name: Scheduler API
components.responsibility: Accepts job creation/cancellation; validates cron expressions; writes to

components.name: Job DB (PostgreSQL)
components.responsibility: Stores job definitions; source of truth for job state

components.name: Redis Sorted Set
components.responsibility: Time-partitioned queue: score = next_run_timestamp; O(log N) enqueue/dequeue

components.name: Scheduler Leader
components.responsibility: Elected via Zookeeper; dequeues due jobs; dispatches to Kafka worker queues

components.name: Kafka Worker Queues
components.responsibility: Separate topics per priority (HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW); workers consume from these

components.name: Worker Pool
components.responsibility: Stateless workers; pull jobs from Kafka; execute with timeout; report results

components.name: Execution DB
components.responsibility: (Cassandra) Stores execution history; partition key = job_id; clustering key = started_at

components.name: Heartbeat Monitor
components.responsibility: Detects timed-out executions; re-queues failed jobs with backoff

questions.title: 2. Handling Missed Jobs
questions.content:
If the scheduler is down for 10 minutes, jobs scheduled during that window are missed. On restart, the
scheduler scans the Redis sorted set for jobs with next_run_timestamp < NOW() and executes them
immediately (catch-up mode). For jobs where missing is acceptable (e.g., metrics aggregation), a 'skip_if_late'
flag prevents catch-up execution.

questions.title: 3. Worker Failure Detection
questions.content:
Workers send heartbeats to the Heartbeat Monitor every 30 seconds. If a worker misses 3 heartbeats, it's
marked as failed. The Heartbeat Monitor queries the Execution DB for running executions on the failed worker
and re-queues them (with retry count incremented). The distributed lock on the execution_id prevents the
original worker (if it recovers) from completing the execution after it's been re-queued.

questions.title: 4. Job Prioritization
questions.content:
Jobs are dispatched to separate Kafka topics per priority level. Workers are configured to consume from HIGH
priority first, then MEDIUM, then LOW. This ensures high-priority jobs are never starved by low-priority jobs. For
burst scenarios, additional worker instances can be spun up for specific priority levels using auto-scaling based
on Kafka consumer lag.

questions.title: 5. Distributed Cron Precision
questions.content:
The scheduler dequeues jobs every 100ms (10 times per second). This gives a scheduling precision of
±100ms, which is sufficient for most use cases. For jobs requiring sub-second precision, the scheduler can be
configured to dequeue every 10ms, but this increases Redis load. True millisecond precision requires a
different architecture (e.g., timer wheels).

keyFlows: ① Client creates job → Job API → Job DB + Redis SortedSet (ZADD)
② Scheduler Leader (Zookeeper elected) polls Redis every 100ms
③ Due jobs dispatched to Kafka (HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW priority topics)
④ Workers consume Kafka → execute with distributed lock → write to Cassandra
⑤ Heartbeat Monitor detects failures → re-queues timed-out executions

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study Zookeeper's leader election protocol: how ephemeral nodes and watches are used to
implement distributed locks and leader election, and the trade-off vs. etcd.
Expert Note: Learn about timer wheels: how they provide O(1) job scheduling by organizing jobs into
circular buckets, and why they're used in kernel-level timers and Kafka's delayed message queue.
Expert Note: Understand Kafka consumer groups: how partition assignment works, how rebalancing is
triggered, and how to minimize rebalancing latency for job scheduling workloads.
Expert Note: Study the exactly-once semantics problem in distributed systems: why it's fundamentally
impossible without idempotency at the application layer, and how to design idempotent job handlers.
Expert Note: Learn about cron expression parsing: the Quartz cron format (7 fields vs. Unix 5 fields), how
to compute the next N execution times efficiently, and edge cases like DST transitions.

Distributed
